nodes.name: Unit Square
nodes.description: The base unit of measure for all constructions. A square of side length 1, held constant by Shift-constraining. All other numbers are defined relative to this unit.

steps: 1. Draw a rectangle while holding SHIFT to constrain it to a perfect square.
2. Define the side length of this square as 1.

nodes.name: Addition (a + b)
nodes.description: Construct the sum of two constructible lengths by placing them end-to-end along a reference line.

steps: 1. Draw an infinite reference line.
2. Place a square of size a aligned to the start of the line.
3. Snap a square of size b to the right edge of a.
4. The total span from the left edge of a to the right edge of b has length a + b.

nodes.name: Subtraction (a − b)
nodes.description: Construct the difference of two constructible lengths. Requires a ≥ b; result is a − b.

steps: 1. Align a square of size b inside a square of size a, snapping their left edges.
2. The remaining gap on the right has length a − b.
3. Snap a new square to this remaining gap.

nodes.name: Multiplication (a × b)
nodes.description: Construct the product of two constructible lengths using the similarity/proportional scaling method. Groups the unit square with b, then scales so the unit snaps to a.

steps: 1. Place a unit square and a square of size b side by side.
2. Select both and group them (Ctrl+G).
3. Resize the group proportionally (Shift-drag) until the unit square snaps to size a.
4. The other square in the group now has side length a × b.

nodes.name: Division (a ÷ b)
nodes.description: Construct the ratio of two constructible lengths using the inverse of the multiplication scaling method.

steps: 1. Place a square of size b and a square of size a side by side.
2. Group both squares.
3. Resize the group proportionally until the b square snaps to the unit square size.
4. The a square now has side length a/b.

nodes.name: Rational Numbers (ℚ)
nodes.description: All rational numbers are constructible. Integers are built by repeated addition of the unit square; fractions by dividing two integers. Since the constructible numbers are closed under +, −, ×, ÷, we have ℚ ⊆ C.

nodes.name: Square Root (√x)
nodes.description: Construct √x for any constructible x using the circle-rectangle intersection method. Discovered as an early key result in the challenge.

steps: 1. Stretch a unit square upward to form a 1×x rectangle.
2. Place a circle whose diameter equals x + 1, centered on the bottom edge so it snaps to both left and right edges of the rectangle.
3. Use Merge Shapes > Intersect to obtain the chord length.
4. The resulting segment has length √x.

nodes.name: Euclidean Constructible Numbers (𝕂)
nodes.description: The Euclidean closure of ℚ — the smallest field extension closed under square roots. This is the classical compass-and-straightedge constructible set.

nodes.name: Cube Root (∛x)
nodes.description: Construct ∛x for any constructible x via intersection of a circle and ellipse with radii derived from x. Found by popop614. The intersection's y-coordinate satisfies (y³ − x)(y − 2) = 0, so y = ∛x. Proves PowerPoint is strictly more powerful than compass-and-straightedge since ∛2 ∉ 𝕂.

steps: 2. Construct a rectangle of width 1/2 with squares of size x/2 + 1 and x/4 + 3/4 aligned.
3. Center a circle of radius R on the upper-right corner of the (x/2 + 1) square.
4. Center an ellipse with semi-axes a and b on the upper-left corner of the (x/4 + 3/4) square.
5. Intersect the circle and ellipse (use a red reference line if needed for snapping to the vertex).
6. The height of the intersection point is ∛x.
7. Proof: intersection satisfies y⁴ − 2y³ − xy + 2x = 0, which factors as (y³ − x)(y − 2) = 0.

nodes.name: Arbitrary Bézier Curve
nodes.description: Construct an arbitrary cubic Bézier curve with constructible control points (P0, P1, P2, P3). Found by Arglin Kampling. Uses reference triangles scaled by 6× and 3×, then Merge Shapes > Union and Smooth Point editing. Enables construction of any algebraic curve expressible as a cubic Bézier.

steps: 1. Construct squares snapped to control points P0, P1, P2, P3 with upper-right corners at the control points.
2. Compute U' = P3 + 6(P0 − P1): construct reference triangle for P1 using vector P1→P0 scaled ×6 from P3.
3. Compute V' = P3 + 3(P3 − P2): construct reference triangle for P2 using vector P2→P3 scaled ×3 from P3.
4. Add supporting shapes between the reference triangles, then apply Merge Shapes > Union.
5. Right-click the result → Edit Points.
6. Right-click the left control handle → Smooth Point.
7. Right-click the right control handle → Smooth Point.
8. Cull remaining vertices as needed.
9. Derivation: U' and V' follow from PowerPoint's Smooth Point handle-averaging behavior for handles spaced 1/3 along each segment.

nodes.name: Parabola (y = x²)
nodes.description: Construct the parabola y = x² on [0,1] using the Bézier method. Found by Arglin Kampling. Control points: A=(0,0), B=(1/3,0), C=(2/3,1/3), D=(1,1). To extend to [0,n], scale so height = width². Can be mirrored/rotated to yield y = √x.

nodes.name: Cubic Curve (y = x³)
nodes.description: Construct the cubic y = x³ on [0,1] using the Bézier method. Found by Arglin Kampling. Control points: A=(0,0), B=(1/3,0), C=(2/3,0), D=(1,1). To extend to [0,n], scale so height = width³. Can be mirrored/rotated to yield y = ∛x.

nodes.name: Quintic Root (⁵√k)
nodes.description: Construct ⁵√k for any constructible k. Found by queuequeue and Arglin Kampling. Graph x = y³/√k and y = x²; their intersection satisfies y⁵ = k, so the y-coordinate is ⁵√k.

nodes.name: Septic Root (⁷√k)
nodes.description: Construct ⁷√k for any constructible k. Found by queuequeue and Arglin Kampling. Construct x² = y³ via Bézier, intersect with y = x³/k. The intersection height is k^(2/7); taking the square root yields ⁷√k.

nodes.name: Trig Functions of Rational Angles
nodes.description: Construct any trig function of any rational multiple of a full turn (2πk/n). Found by sunny_lu_89 via SmartArt. Places n equally-spaced circles using Insert > SmartArt > Cycle > Basic Cycle, extracts cot(πk/n) from rectangle proportions, then derives all other trig values.

steps: 1. Insert > SmartArt > Cycle > Basic Cycle. Add/remove shapes until there are n circles.
2. Select the topmost circle and the k-th circle counterclockwise. Copy both outside the SmartArt.
3. Draw lines snapping to the pasted circles, then snap a rectangle's corners to the tops of the lines.
4. Scale the rectangle so its height matches a unit square. Width is now cot(πk/n).
5. Derive remaining functions: tan = 1/cot, sec = √(tan² + 1), cos = 1/sec, sin = cos·tan, csc = 1/sin.
6. Proof: the n circles are spaced at 2πk/n; half-angle analysis gives cot(πk/n) as the width/height ratio.

nodes.name: Inverse Trig Functions (arcsin, arccos, arctan)
nodes.description: Construct inverse trig functions for any constructible input. Noted as 'obvious the entire time' on 21 Feb 2026 — follows directly from the ability to construct trig values and reverse the geometric construction.

nodes.name: Pi (π)
nodes.description: Construct π via circumference scaling. A circle of diameter 1 has circumference π; this arc length is extracted and used as a constructible length. Proven constructible 2 Dec 2025.

nodes.name: One Radian (1 rad)
nodes.description: Construct an angle of exactly 1 radian. Proven constructible 2 Dec 2025 alongside π.

nodes.name: Natural Logarithm (ln a)
nodes.description: Construct ln(a) for any constructible a > 0 using the arc length of a parabolic segment. This follows from the specific identity ln(a) = ((a²-1)/a)S + (1-a⁴)/(4a²), where S is the arc length of y = ((a²-1)/4a)x² on [0,1].

steps: 1. Construct the unit parabola y = x² on the interval [0,1].
2. Calculate the factor k = (a² - 1) / (4a) using constructible arithmetic operations.
3. Vertically stretch the unit parabola by the factor k (adjusting height relative to width).
4. Measure the arc length S of the resulting curve using the EMF decomposition method (arc_length).
5. Combine S and a using the formula ln(a) = ((a² - 1) / a) * S + (1 - a⁴) / (4a²) using arithmetic operations.

nodes.name: Logarithm (log_a b)
nodes.description: Construct the logarithm base a of b using the change of base formula: log_a b = ln(b) / ln(a).

nodes.name: Base-2 Exponentiation (2^x)
nodes.description: Construct 2^x for any constructible x using PowerPoint's exponential trendline feature. By fitting a trendline to the points (0,1) and (1,2) in a bar chart and extracting the curve via EMF, we obtain the function 2^x.

steps: 1. Insert a Bar Chart and use the Excel data to create two bars with heights 1 and 2 at positions 0 and 1.
2. Add an 'Exponential Trendline' to the data series.
3. Copy the chart and use Paste Special (Enhanced Metafile) to extract the trendline as a vector curve.
4. Ungroup the EMF and isolate the trendline curve.
5. Scale and position the curve relative to a coordinate system.
6. For any constructible x, use Merge Shapes or snapping to find the height of the curve at that position to obtain 2^x.

nodes.name: General Exponentiation (a^b)
nodes.description: Construct a^b for any constructible a > 0 and b. This is implemented via the identity a^b = 2^(b * log2(a)), where log2(a) is computed via the logarithm node.

nodes.name: Euler's Number (e)
nodes.description: Construct e via 2^(1/ln 2). Since 2^(1/ln 2) = e^(ln 2 / ln 2) = e. First proven constructible 4 Dec 2025 using PowerPoint ACE; proven without ACE 21 Feb 2026.

nodes.name: Perimeter of a Constructible Ellipse
nodes.description: Construct the perimeter of any ellipse with constructible semi-axes. Proven constructible 2 Dec 2025.

nodes.name: Paste Special (Enhanced Metafile)
nodes.description: A core PowerPoint trick to convert vector strokes into individual objects. By pasting a shape as an Enhanced Metafile and ungrouping it, its component parts (like dashes in a line) become separate, selectable shapes.

steps: 1. Select and copy (Ctrl+C) any object or group.
2. Go to Paste > Paste Special (or Ctrl+Alt+V).
3. Choose 'Picture (Enhanced Metafile)' and click OK.
4. Ungroup the result (Ctrl+Shift+G) twice to convert it into vector objects.

nodes.name: Arc Length
nodes.description: Construct the arc length of a curve using the EMF decomposition method. Discovered 17 Feb 2026. This allows measuring curved paths by breaking them into segments.

steps: 1. Select the curve and apply a dashed outline style.
2. Copy the curve and use Paste Special (Enhanced Metafile).
3. Ungroup twice to turn the dashes into individual line segments.
4. Align the resulting segments end-to-end to measure the total length.

nodes.name: Lemniscate Constant (ϖ)
nodes.description: Construct the lemniscate constant ϖ = 2∫₀¹ dt/√(1−t⁴), the arc length of the lemniscate of Bernoulli. Proven constructible 18 Feb 2026.

nodes.name: Gamma Function at Integer Quarters (Γ(n/4))
nodes.description: Construct Γ(n/4) for any integer n. Proven constructible 17 Feb 2026 as a consequence of arc length constructibility.

nodes.name: Power Regression (Chart Curve Fitting)
nodes.description: Use PowerPoint's built-in chart power regression to fit a curve y = ax^b to constructible data points. Discovered 28 Feb 2026. Enables arbitrary septic and octic roots with rational coefficients, and is the mechanism behind general exponentiation a^b.

nodes.name: Octic Root (⁸√k)
nodes.description: Construct ⁸√k for any constructible k with rational coefficients using the power regression feature. Confirmed constructible 28 Feb 2026.
